title: Paper Explained: Taming Momentum: Rethinking Optimizer States Through Low-Rank Approximation - A Beginner's Guide
subtitle: Smarter Training with Less Memory for Big AI Models

content.background:
Big language models are incredibly powerful, but training them has a hidden cost: the optimizers themselves need a lot of memory. Modern optimizers keep track of not only the current gradients but also their past behavior (two kinds of momentum). For every parameter in a huge model, the optimizer stores extra numbers to remember this history. As models grow from millions to billions of parameters, this per-parameter memory adds up to a huge extra load on hardware. That means you either need more expensive GPUs, you have to shrink the model or the batch size, or you slow down training because you’re juggling more data to fit in memory. In short, the memory footprint of the optimizer becomes a real bottleneck for scaling.

Think of it like packing for a trip. If you’re traveling with a tiny backpack, you can fit everything you need easily. But as your trip grows and you bring along more gear (in this case, the optimizer’s history for every parameter), suddenly you’re carrying a massive load. The heavier your “backpack,” the less room you have for other essentials, and the slower your journey becomes. This is especially painful when researchers want to pre-train or fine-tune giant language models efficiently, test many ideas, or push scaling to new frontiers. The memory cost of maintaining optimization state is a practical limit that slows progress and raises costs.

Against this backdrop, there’s a broader push in AI research to make training more memory-efficient without sacrificing performance. Techniques that compress or simplify large computations—like using low-rank representations to capture essential structure with far fewer numbers—are already popular for reducing model size and compute. This paper asks: can we rethink the optimizer’s own memory in the same spirit? By framing the optimizer’s accumulated information as something that can be stored more compactly, the work aims to let us train and tune bigger models more cheaply and efficiently, broadening what’s feasible on standard hardware and enabling faster experimentation across model sizes.
content.methodology:
Momentum-based optimizers like Adam or Muon keep track of past gradient information (first and second moments) to decide how to update every parameter. For huge models, storing and updating this momentum state becomes a major memory burden, limiting scalability. The key idea of this paper is to look at that momentum not as a big, separate store of data, but as the behavior of a tiny online learner—a linear regressor that predicts parameter updates from recent gradients. In other words, EMA (the moving average) is reinterpreted as the training dynamics of a small, online linear model. Building on that view, the authors propose LoRA-Pre, a low-rank optimizer that keeps only a compact subspace of the full momentum state, dramatically cutting memory needs while preserving performance.

How LoRA-Pre works conceptually (step-by-step, in plain terms):
- Treat momentum as an online prediction problem: the optimizer is learning a tiny linear predictor that maps incoming gradients to the appropriate update amounts.
- Replace the full, high-dimensional momentum with a low-rank representation: instead of storing a large matrix, it stores two small factors that multiply to approximate the momentum directions we care about.
- Update in real time with a small subspace: as new gradients arrive, the method updates those two small factors so the predictor continues to capture the most useful directions for updating parameters.
- Apply this during pre-training and fine-tuning: LoRA-Pre uses the same small-rank representation during pre-training and then leverages the same rank when fine-tuning, showing strong performance across tasks.

What they achieved, and why it matters:
- Pre-training on Llama-family models from 60M to 1B parameters: LoRA-Pre delivers the best performance across all model sizes, despite using a much smaller rank for the optimizer state.
- Rank efficiency: the method reaches competitive or better results with only about 1/8 the rank of baseline approaches, meaning far less memory spent on optimizer state.
- Fine-tuning gains: with the same low rank, LoRA-Pre outperforms efficient fine-tuning baselines, delivering noticeable improvements (for example, 3.14 points on Llama-3.1-8B and 6.17 points on Llama-2-7B).
- Practical impact: the approach suggests a scalable path to training and adapting very large models without the hefty memory burden of traditional momentum states, and the authors even provide public code to reproduce their results.

In short, LoRA-Pre reimagines how optimizer memories are stored: by viewing momentum as an online, low-rank linear learner, it keeps only the essential directions of update, cutting memory usage substantially while delivering strong performance in both pre-training and fine-tuning.
content.results:
This paper tackles a practical bottleneck in training huge language models: the memory typically needed to store and update the optimizer’s momentum terms. The authors show that the common EMA-based momentum used by optimizers like Adam can be viewed as online learning of a simple linear model. Building on that idea, they design LoRA-Pre, a low-rank optimizer that keeps the same optimization behavior but stores momentum in a compact, low-rank form. In plain terms, they replace a big, chunky memory footprint with a slim, factorized representation, so you can get the same guidance for how to adjust model weights while using much less memory.

In experiments, LoRA-Pre shines in both pre-training and fine-tuning. They pre-train a family of models inspired by Llama, ranging from relatively small to quite large, and LoRA-Pre consistently achieves top performance across all sizes. A standout point is its “rank efficiency”: it attains similar or better results using only a fraction (about one-eighth) of the rank that baseline methods require. This means you can get the same or better training quality with far less memory dedicated to the optimizer state. For fine-tuning, the story is equally favorable: with the same compact rank, LoRA-Pre outperforms other efficient fine-tuning methods, and it also shows clear gains over standard LoRA on several model sizes. All of this is demonstrated across multiple model scales, suggesting the approach generalizes well from smaller to larger families.

The practical impact is meaningful: you can train and fine-tune large language models more efficiently, with lower memory pressure, which translates to lower hardware costs and the potential to scale to bigger models or researchers with fewer resources. Because the method stays competitive (and often superior) even with a much smaller memory footprint, it makes large-model work more accessible and affordable. The work also provides an open-source implementation, inviting the community to try, test, and build on it with real-world models.
content.significance:
This paper matters today because it tackles a practical bottleneck of training giant language models: memory usage. Modern optimizers like Adam keep track of momentum and curvature information, which grows with the model size and the training steps. By showing that the EMA-based momentum can be viewed as online gradient flow training of a linear regressor, and then replacing the full momentum with a compact, low-rank representation (LoRA-Pre), the authors offer a way to cut memory without sacrificing performance. In other words, you can keep training large models with far less memory spent on optimizer state, which is a big win when hardware is a limiting factor.

In the long run, this line of work could reshape how we design and train AI systems. It pushes toward “memory-light” optimizer design, which pairs naturally with other efficiency tricks like low-rank adapters and sparsity. If optimizer states can be compressed to a small subspace while preserving optimization quality, researchers can push to train even bigger models, run more extensive hyperparameter searches, or deploy continual or domain-specific fine-tuning with lower costs. This also opens doors for more energy-efficient training pipelines and makes it easier for research labs and companies with modest compute to experiment with state-of-the-art architectures.

For modern AI systems people know—think ChatGPT-like assistants, Copilots, and other large-language-model services—the impact is practical and timely. Lowering the memory footprint of the optimizer can speed up pretraining and fine-tuning cycles, enabling faster iteration and more frequent updates to deployed models. Because LoRA-Pre emphasizes high rank efficiency, it aligns with the broader industry trend of using low-rank adapters and compact representations to customize models quickly for new domains or languages. In short, this work helps make large AI models cheaper to train and easier to adapt, a capability that will matter as we continue to scale AI systems and demand more responsible, flexible, and accessible AI technology.

concept_explanation.title: Understanding Low-rank Optimizer: The Heart of Taming Momentum
concept_explanation.content:
Think of training a giant neural network like steering a huge ship. Each parameter is a little knob you can turn, and the optimizer is your autopilot that decides how hard to push each knob next. In popular optimizers like Adam, the autopilot keeps two big memories (first and second moments) about how the knobs have moved in the past. That memory helps you decide which directions to push now, but it also takes up a lot of space. The paper asks a simple question: what if that big memory isn’t stored as two separate, per-parameter lists, but is instead learned as a compact, low-precision “subspace” that still captures the main patterns of motion?

Here’s how it works, step by step, in plain terms. First, the authors reinterpret the exponential moving average (EMA) used in momentum-based optimizers as if you were training a tiny online linear learner. Instead of just keeping a history of gradients, you think of the momentum as a linear operator that maps the current gradient to the update you should apply. This makes the momentum sound like a small predictive model operating inside the optimizer. From there, they notice a natural way to compress this operator: represent it as a low-rank matrix. A full momentum operator is big; a low-rank version is built from a few small building blocks, like a few key directions in which updates mostly flow, plus how much to mix those directions. In short, you replace a large, dense state with a compact, learnable set of basis directions.

To make the idea concrete, imagine the momentum as a matrix W that acts on the gradient g to produce the update: update ∝ W g. In standard setups, you’d store W explicitly and apply it every step. In LoRA-Pre, you factor W as W ≈ U V^T, where U and V are much smaller than W but can still express the important update directions. During training, the optimizer learns U and V online as gradients come in, and when you need the actual update you compute it as U (V^T g). This keeps the effective behavior of momentum while avoiding the need to store a huge matrix. The key idea is that most of the useful momentum lives in a few dominant directions; the low-rank factors capture those directions with far less memory.

Why is this important, especially for large language models? Training models with billions of parameters already stretches memory and compute. The standard momentum state scales with the model size, so memory can become a bottleneck. A low-rank optimizer like LoRA-Pre aims to keep similar optimization performance while saving memory by focusing on a small set of important directions in which the momentum acts. The paper reports that, in pre-training and fine-tuning experiments with Llama-family models (from tens of millions up to around a billion parameters), LoRA-Pre achieves comparable or better results using a fraction of the rank, meaning you get similar quality updates with much smaller internal state. This is what they mean by “rank efficiency.”

Practically, this opens up several useful applications. If you’re training huge models but with limited memory, a low-rank optimizer can let you run longer or scale to bigger architectures without needing as much memory devoted to optimizer state. It also makes fine-tuning more efficient: with the same small rank, LoRA-Pre consistently outperforms standard efficient fine-tuning baselines in their tests. Since the method is designed to work in both pre-training and fine-tuning, it could enable researchers and engineers to iterate faster on large-model projects, deploy on hardware with tighter memory budgets, or experiment with larger curricula. The authors also share their code publicly, so you can try the idea on your own models and datasets.

In short, the “Low-rank Optimizer” idea reframes how momentum is stored and updated: instead of a full, high-dimensional memory, you learn a compact, low-rank representation that captures the essential directions of momentum. The practical payoff is clear in large-scale experiments—similar performance with far less internal state, enabling more scalable pre-training and efficient fine-tuning. If you can explain momentum as a small, online linear learner and then compress that learner into a few directions, you’ve got a powerful, memory-friendly path to training giant neural networks.

summary: This paper introduces LoRA-Pre, a low-rank optimizer that turns optimizer momentum into a small subspace within an online linear learner to dramatically reduce memory while maintaining or improving performance in pre-training and fine-tuning of large language models.
